Transcrição Completa: 88 How to Handle a Sales Teams That Don't Perform 6 9 24
[00:00] All right, sweet. So, we had a request this week for our uh group call. Um, number one, Shane asked, "My top sales closer is kind of in a slump right now. He's not been performing up to his normal closing percentage. Jeremy, can you cover on tomorrow's call how to boost morale of the sales team going through the slump and reignite the flame under them?"
[00:24] So, to be clear, it's a little broader topic than just that because I want to cover how to make sure that the sales team stays locked in and selling as consistently as they can. That way, you avoid slumps entirely. All the advice I'm going to give is very practical for getting them out of a slump as well.
[00:42] Uh most of you obviously compartmentalize me as a marketer and that's okay because you know that's technically what I am. However, it's also important to note that I am deeply involved in most of the sales organizations that we work with because them not closing as much as they could will fuck up my amount of money I can make.
[01:02] So therefore, I give a huge huge huge fuck about the salespeople, the amount of revenue that they're bringing into the organization, their show rates, their close rates. Um, as most of you know, I've developed an entire sales training library that, you know, if you don't have access to it, DM me after this and I'll make sure you get into it.
[01:22] Um, we give that away as well to all of your sales teams that you guys are working with just to add some additional value to what you guys do. Uh, I have SOPs on show rate and connection rate mastery that we've typed up. I'll do sales training calls in some instances with the organizations that we work with.
[01:38] And keep in mind, I have been in sales myself for the last 11 years. Um, obviously running my own business and closing huge deals and taking everything I'm about to teach you and implementing it into my own organization as well. So, I feel quite qualified to be able to talk to you guys about this and hand down some really solid lessons.
[01:58] Um, to be clear, I do have uh a new setup today, so excuse me if I have any hiccups throughout the process. I am learning alongside of you today on how to use this new thing. I got that new iPad. So, we're going to see how it works together.
[02:12] Um, to be clear, this is a very important set of lessons that we are going to go through today. Um, I just want to jump right into it. And to be clear, when we get started with all of this, um, it's very important to note there's a few key things that drive consistency from a sales team and that can bring them out of a slump if you're experiencing that.
[02:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ACCOUNTABILITY"
NÚMEROS: #1
VISUAL: Título em destaque, primeira posição numerada, ênfase visual no conceito de responsabilização
[02:35] So number one is accountability. This might sound simple but this is one of the most undermined elements of a sales organization. A lot of people would prefer that as time continues to pass they can become more handsoff in the organization.
[02:51] So typically what leaders will do like take what Andrew was just saying as one of his weekly wins or take what uh take what uh Nick Mason said or take what Tampa Nicks said. Um, all of these individuals commented on the fact that as leaders within their organizations, they staffed individuals.
[03:10] They trained up these individuals because it's going to help them in scaling up the business. It's going to take a load off of them. It's going to take a burden off of them and allow them to hand that off to somebody else.
[03:21] Now, for a short period of time, could be months, for some people, it could be weeks, they're going to be at a very intense, heightened stage of accountability where over a short duration of time, they're going to experience an increase in the amount of day-to-day conversations they're going to have with an individual on whether they're hitting specific KPIs, whether they're doing what they need to do.
[03:44] Getting them all the training, resource, access that they need, potentially buying other program, programs, courses, uh, putting that individual into somebody else's program, like a sales training course as an example, and simply put, trying to skill that person up and holding them accountable to being great, so they don't waste their money on that new hire, and so they maximize the revenue that can come from that individual.
[04:09] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Accountability Over Time"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando linha descendente de accountability ao longo do tempo, contraste entre expectativa vs realidade, representação visual de declínio
[04:11] The downside is is that how most business owners and leaders operate is as time continues to pass, the accountability goes down because they naturally will will want to become more handsoff and they want things to become autonomous, you know, and they want things to become less energy and effort intensive.
[04:31] You know, ideally what you've what you've likely been fooled into thinking is that as time continues to pass, the leader of the organization can become more and more and more handsoff. Okay.
[04:43] Uh most recently, I've been reading this thick biblicalsized book from Walter Isaac on Elon Musk. There are some incredible stories in there of how intense Elon is as a leader. And one of the most consistent elements to almost every single story within that book of how Elon operates is he is up everybody's ass in his organization.
[05:05] Down to the people who are working like Tesla customer support to the rocket scientists that are building literal rocket ships to the engineering teams that work at Twitter now X. U he is up everybody's ass. He is in no way, shape or form a leader that is just kicking his feet up, you know, sitting back, letting all these other smart, really highly intelligent, highly self-accountable individuals just do things on their own.
[05:30] He is to a degree almost, I would call it micromanaging, but it's, you know, he doesn't have the time to obviously micromanage to the level that I, you know, most of us would think of it as. Simply put, he is the opposite of hands off. Okay.
[05:45] One of my old bosses, Grant Cardone, he had a great video where he was talking about how up everybody's ass he was within his organization. How once again from an accountability perspective, if something goes wrong, rather than just sitting there, you know, oh, letting everybody admit it as if it didn't happen, he'll find the exact individual that did it, you know, make sure that he teaches them the lesson, potentially reprimands them, and ensures once again that that mistake won't happen again.
[06:13] Or if something positive happens, you know, he'll obviously go out of his way to make sure that that's acknowledged, praised, you know, and repeated again. I can tell you from working there firsthand, he is the exact opposite of hands off. Uh he is one of the most accountable leaders that I have ever worked under in my entire career that I've ever seen and witnessed and been able to experience firsthand.
[06:37] Um what's very interesting to me is there's all these people who make like very little money. like they make like maybe 100 to like 200k a month or less in most instances and they get this idea that they can just you know fuck off and act like they don't need to technically like vamp up the amount of once again accountability in this case throughout the organization.

Okay, this makes sales people need daily accountability.
[07:23] They deal with rejection more than anybody else in terms of career rejection. Okay, potentially hundreds of people a day. In most instances, dozens of people a day and some days, depending on the type of salesperson, just a handful of rejections a day. Um, ideally more, but you know, some of them deal with less.

Okay, this is critical for the success development ongoing potential being actualized from a salesperson within an organization.
[08:06] This this includes to be clear sales management. There is nobody within the sales organization that excludes that is excluded from this structure. Okay, they have to be held accountable and they have to have a structure. So what does structure look like?
[08:21] So back in the day uh once again if I use Grant's organization I came into that business there was 25 salespeople every day before they showed up there was daily sales training that would happen before they were even allowed into the office.
[08:36] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Daily Sales Training - Cardone University"
NÚMEROS: 25 salespeople, 6 videos diários, 20-30 minutos, centenas/milhares de vídeos
VISUAL: Estrutura de treinamento matinal, layout de processo pré-trabalho, ícones de educação e desenvolvimento
[08:38] Okay. Now this was done through Cardone University which was obviously without exaggeration it might have been thousands of videos but at a minimum it was several several hundred like high hundreds and an individual within grand sales team had to watch before they walked into the office six training videos.
[08:58] Okay that was that was a very uh easy accomplishment. six videos might have been like, you know, a one minute to five minute video, you know, nothing crazy, but maybe like 20 to 30 minutes in the morning set aside towards sales training and just developing themselves.
[09:14] Um, it's very important to understand that the data that a salesperson consistently gets exposed to in a in a recent duration of time affects what they're going to be able to use and deploy into sales conversations that they're having.
[09:28] So, without that, you lose you you essentially are are operating on like recency bias. Sales people operate 100% on recency bias. So, you want to make sure that you constantly are are making them focus on things that are new, that are little like quirky things they could say on calls to overcome objections that are just like strategies in general.
[09:50] Um, you know, once again, it's best practices. You want to make sure you're constantly reiterating these things. This is why daily sales training is necessary. And believe it or not, it doesn't always have to be something different.
[10:02] This is a very surprising thing about salespeople is I can tell them, "Hey, I need you to go send selfie videos to people to increase your show rate upwards of 30%." And they'll they'll give I'll give them an example. I'll show them exactly how to do it. They'll go out and they'll do it the next like four or five days.

NÚMEROS: 30% aumento no show rate, 2-3 semanas de duração
VISUAL: Exemplo de tática específica, representação de resultado positivo, timeline de implementação e abandono
[10:21] They'll report back to me that they're getting an incredible result by doing so. And all of a sudden, they're just not fucking doing it anymore like two to three weeks later. So, this really goes to show how important it is to have consistency in daily sales training and and potentially repeating lessons.
[10:38] Um, it's very important to understand like I don't I don't ever feel like I need to develop new ongoing stuff for sales people because a lot of what consistently works is just the things that are already proven. There's very few like new random things that are going to come out come about in sales training.

[12:44] And this is this is also very important to understand structurally for their day-to-day. Right after that, they'd go into role play. Okay? And they would role play for about 30 minutes. They'd break into pods of about two to four people and they would sit there and they would just, you know, go through what kind of uh objections they were dealing with the previous day.
[13:05] You know, they'd like start a sales call from the top. They did a fuckload of cold calling in Grant's business. So, to be clear, they would roleplay with the types of calls that they would go through from cold calls to follow-up calls to demo calls. Like, they would do all of them and they would do that in about 20 to 30 minutes.

[13:39] There is a there is a really high probability that when you put people through all of this that they are going to have a higher probability to go out there and win big versus how are your sales people right now starting their day.

[14:56] But at the same time, I mean, dude, it is a horrendous way to start the day if you start off terribly. But the opposite is also true. You know, Leo, if you if you do really good in the morning, does it positively affect the rest of your day?

And dude, it can drastically impact the success or the downfall of that specific day and the momentum that we experience right off the rip. Okay?
[15:30] So, it's very important to note when you have full control over your sales organization and once again, you're just actively considering what can I do to get the momentum started immediately before they even go out there and take a single call before they follow up with anybody rather than just rolling out of bed and just fucking sitting down at the computer and like taking calls.
[15:51] You really got to take control, you know? You got to assume control. Okay? And this is a very important thing to note as well. I would consider it an entirely separate point.
[16:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "ASSUME CONTROL OVER PERSONAL LIVES" / "#3"

[16:04] Brandon Carter talked to us about this at a mastermind recently where he said he assumes control and responsibility over their personal lives. Now, some people might consider this taking it a step too far. Okay? But allow me to break down what Brandon specifically does here so we're all on the same page with how serious he takes this.
[16:25] He will track their macros. Okay? If everybody to be clear is not familiar with Brandon Carter, I'm sure we all are. But let me just articulate how he works. If you go to a restaurant, Brandon, he brings a food scale. He will weigh any singular thing that he consumes in his body, measure it, and ensure that is tracked inside of the My Fitness Pal.
[16:47] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Brandon Carter's System"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Exemplo de sistema de tracking completo, ícones de nutrição e accountability pessoal, conceito de controle total
[16:49] He has his salespeople do the exact same stuff. He tracks their exact calorie count, their macros. Um, he tracks their personal lives and their relationships. So, as an example, one of his longest term best salespeople, his name's Brian. Brian's recently gone through a dry spell.
[17:07] You know, simply put, he's not he's not not closing deals, but his statistics have fallen tremendously. And, you know, Brandon has a call after about a week and a half of noticing this this drop off in statistics like close rate, show rate, etc. It's never random. You know, there's always a reason.
[17:26] And he finds out, well, Brian's going through a breakup. you know, Brian's girlfriend of a long-term period of time. They're going through a rocky period, you know, and they're essentially in the process of breaking up. And this is this is once again really drastically impacted Brian's performance and therefore Brandon's revenue as a result of this.
[17:45] So Brandon doesn't allow that that kind of stuff to just slide by and occur. Brandon, when I say he assumes control over their personal lives, this is important to understand because I don't want to make it sound like it's a demeaning thing or something that's like overstepping.
[18:01] You know, it's technically a very responsible thing to do. He'll simply put step in as a mentor in this example and ensure that Brian has somebody to talk to and that he can work through that cycle faster than he would if it was just on its own.
[18:16] I'm sure that you guys can all reflect there's likely somebody in your life at some point who you either know or maybe you've gone through this yourself unfortunately where that kind of you know circumstance happening of a breakup can really negatively impact you for a long duration of time if you if you don't have anybody to help you rebound out of that situation and have people friends you know just people you can talk to.
[18:38] So Brandon once again he assumes that role he assumes that position he ensures that once again his people are not going to be, you know, fucked up essentially and having something in their life ruin the potential of the organization and the business and themselves to be fair.
[18:55] Um, to be clear, just a few more comments on structure real quick before I move off of that. Uh, every salesperson needs to have a set of KPIs that they hit within a day and that they meet as like a minimum expectation.

[19:11] And this is important to note. It's technically its own point, but it technically still relates under structure. Uh to be clear, KPIs, this would include show rate. Uh this would include like, you know, minimum minimum amount of follow-ups.
[19:26] This would include, as an example, if they're doing like cold outreach or or farming, you know, like x x amount of uh outreach per day. This could also be as an example you know minimum close rates there has to be expectations okay because without expectations there's nothing to meet and exceed.
[19:46] And this is what's important to note when I say that this heavily relates to structure okay and that this in itself is very related to just the mental stance of a salesperson without an expectation to meet or exceed there's no boundary to operate within. Okay.
[20:03] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Grant Cardone's KPI Wall"
NÚMEROS: Estatísticas semanais impressas

[20:05] So, if I go back to my time at Grant's office, what was very interesting there is on a week- overweek basis on his wall, Grant would print out the statistics of every individual salesperson's KPI that they tracked. Okay?
[20:20] And for them, as I mentioned, they were a very like cold outbound type of organization. So, he would track the amount of calls that they made per day. That was one of the main statistics that was quantified as an important stat that made all the difference to all the other stats.
[20:36] As long as they had x amount of people that they reached out to in a day, it's a very high probability that they would meet and exceed the amount of revenue that they were expected to produce as a salesperson. So that was one of the main stats they tracked.

That way you don't have to track all of them and then hold them hold them accountable to everything. To be clear, you do still want to track everything. It's important to note, but regardless, you know, there is a set or a main stat that really impacts everything else.
[21:16] So to be clear once again, the salespeople would have a stat that they had to meet or exceed on a day-to-day basis. And if they failed within a week to meet or exceed that statistic, okay? Or even if their graph was trending down, and this is another important thing to note, they would look at it on like a statistical line graph.

[22:07] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Performance Recognition System"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Contraste entre intervenção para baixa performance vs reconhecimento para alta performance, sistema de pedestal para vencedores
[22:09] Vice versa, if the salesperson's statistics were were trending up, that person would be praised. That person wouldn't have to have an individual meeting where they're asked, "Why are you failing?" Quite the opposite. They would actually be put on a pedestal to the rest of the sales team and demonstrate what they're doing well to everybody else, which helps to create momentum within the sales organization. Okay?
[22:33] When you have somebody who's winning big or a group of people that are winning big within the sales team and you have some losers or just some average performers in comparison, articulating what is happening within your winners to the to the average people or the losers raises the bar for everybody else within the organization.
[22:53] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "POSITIVE NARRATIVE" / "#4"
NÚMEROS: #4
VISUAL: Quarto ponto principal destacado, conceito de propaganda positiva, layout de gestão de mentalidade
[22:55] This is what's important to understand. We have to we have to acknowledge that salespeople they need a a positive narrative. Okay, this is very very critical to implement within your team if you want to see constant success.
[23:10] When I say narrative, okay, what do I mean? A narrative is the story that a salesperson and a sales team is telling themselves. As an example, put in the chat real quick, how many of you guys have operated in a sales team or or managed your own sales team or worked with a sales team that has a narrative of like, oh, the lead quality is bad, like people aren't qualified.
[23:32] How well do they do during those times when the sales team's thinking, "Oh, the leads are bad." You know, how well are they closing? They're doing they're dog shit They're terrible when they think that, right?
[23:44] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Narrative Impact: Negative vs Positive"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre narrativa negativa ("leads ruins") e narrativa positiva ("leads quentes"), impacto direto no desempenho
[23:46] But vice versa. Well, what what happens when the opposite is is is mentally in those people's heads where they're thinking, "Dude, lead quality is hot right now. Okay, everybody's doing great. We have a lot of winners in the team that are making a lot of money."
[24:01] How do you perpetuate that type of narrative within your organization? Just take a moment real quick. Do you do any of this within your sales team? When it comes to perpetuating a positive narrative, this creates a tremendous amount of momentum. Okay? And that's very important to understand. This is critical. Okay? This is not an option. This is excessively important.
[24:24] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Positive Salesperson Propaganda"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de propaganda interna positiva, três pilares principais destacados, sistema de reforço de mentalidade
[24:26] Um to be clear, it's it's positive salesperson propaganda. That's all it is. What do sales people need in terms of propaganda? I'd say it's pretty simple. Number one, customers winning. They need to know that there's consistently whatever the fuck that they're selling, people are getting great results with it.
[24:45] People are winning. People are getting the result that they paid your organization to help them get. Okay? They need to know that their fellow salesperson is winning. So if you have more than one person in your team, they need to know that that other person's also doing good because how well does a salesperson do when everybody else around them they think is doing bad?
[25:06] Critical. Okay. And this is once again this is why we share positive propaganda wins other people doing well. We want to prop those people up on a pedestal. We want to share what's going good for those individuals. Okay.
[25:19] To be clear, additional positive propaganda can also just be the culture that they operate within. I constantly comment on that on key teams that I've worked in through the years. Makes all the difference for the productivity of everybody within the organization.
[25:34] How many of you have worked within a team before where you are just fucking pumped to work alongside of the people that you've hired or that you get to work alongside of? I've had businesses that I've gone into where I I feel blessed and honored to be able to work alongside of the people that I'm working alongside of.
[25:51] I would articulate them as smart, highly motivated, happy, super productive, like they they raise the bar for everybody else. Okay? And that's what you want to understand. Your winners need to be propped up within the company.
[26:05] And and it's important once again to ensure that this happens because it plays such a critical role once again for for the average people becoming good and the good people becoming great. Okay, it raises the bar for everybody once again when people are just engulfed in a culture of everybody else around me is doing better than me. I need to do better too. Okay, critical once again for a salesperson.
[26:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Negative Narrative = Death"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Alerta visual sobre perigo de narrativas negativas, representação de impacto destrutivo, conceito de contágio mental
[26:30] To be clear, sales people do horrendous when there is a narrative that's negative. So recently, I'll I'll use Brandon Carter's team again. We had a we had an instance where some of the salespeople started to articulate that lead quality was bad. Okay?
[26:47] When in reality, the statistics were the complete opposite of that. The stats couldn't be further from the truth for what these salespeople were thinking. Everything was good. I And I mean everything was good. They were doing great with the exact with technically with a less qualified person coming in historically.
[27:06] And out of nowhere they just all started because sales people talk amongst themselves. So if one person has that cancerous thought of like and like I said they also operate off recency buys. So if like in the last two days one of the key leaders within the sales team says some shit to the other salespeople like yeah dude I I had some bad quality leads these last like two three days.
[27:28] Bingo. That's what the other fucking sales people are now going to look for, too. And then the other sales people start to see that whether it's real or not. And then once again, they go out of their way to start saying the same thing.
[27:41] And all of a sudden, you've got this fucking loop in your entire sales team of people thinking to themselves, "Well, the lead quality is bad right now. You know, there's nothing I'm going to be able to do. I'm not going to be able to close as many folks because the lead quality is poor." Okay.
[27:56] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Fabricated Video Solution"
NÚMEROS: Vídeo de 2 minutos
VISUAL: Setup de vídeo estratégico, conceito de reframing através de comunicação fabricada, técnica de quebra de narrativa negativa
[27:58] I'm going to share something with you that I did when that recently happened within Brandon Carter's sales team. What I did is I created a video when I was at my other house here. Okay? And I want you I want you to watch this real quick. Okay? It's a two-minute video. Take a moment and really just really just listen to what I say and uh then I'm going to tell you afterwards the importance of this.
[28:21] CLT, what's going on? Hope you guys are feeling well. This is Jeremy. I do the Facebook advertising. Uh one change that I want to let you guys know that we may have few changes to be clear. Bas guys feedback. I know you guys talked a lot about be recently and seems like a struggle a little bit. Become a little less qualified.

[28:43] So we made some pretty significant changed demographic of working professionals. We also geographically targeted major cities. these major cities. [29:04] Simply put, we're targeting people used to spend at least a couple thousand bucks a month on random purchases and simply think that pay will be a lot. So in addition to that, we have random my house here in Miami. You guys might see some of those ads shot film all of the apps.
[29:21] We pulled every previous ad out and we relaunched them with some apps that also specifically talk to that higher financial qualified demographic. um that should also help tremendously. [29:40] Every single thing very clearly articulates the minimum amount of money that they ideally would spend at about $4,000. And we also have people coming through fully aware of the pricing. Once again, targeting has changed, ads themselves have changed, the videos themselves have changed, ad copy has changed, and then the form itself has changed.
[30:00] Should see very significant differences in terms of quality of person the other side of this thing. So just kind of keep your eye out. So long story short pretty significant difference in your ability to be able to close people and your ability to cash more money per person.
[30:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "THE TRUTH: Nothing Changed"
NÚMEROS: 0 mudanças reais
VISUAL: Revelação dramática, contraste entre comunicação vs realidade, exemplo prático de reframing de narrativa
[30:44] Yeah. So, guess what? That was totally fucking made up. I didn't make a single change to anything. I didn't do anything different. I didn't change the targeting. I didn't change the videos. We didn't make any new videos. Okay? We didn't do a fucking thing different. Literally, not a single thing.
[31:03] What do you think the difference was after the salespeople watched that video? They thought there was a difference. They immediately, and I'm talking immediately, Nick, saw a difference. Okay.
[31:17] Like the next the next several days they were like, "Oh, dude, lead quality improved a fuckload." You know, uh people are showing up more. You know, Jeremy, I don't all those changes are working exceptionally well. You know what I mean?
[31:31] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Perception = Reality for Sales Teams"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de realidade perceptual vs realidade estatística, poder da narrativa sobre performance
[31:33] Like I said, statistically, there's not a single fucking thing that changed. Technically everything improved in lead quality and the sales people developed the narrative of well you know just bad leads right now you know and and once again analytically statistically from application data everything was better not worse.
[31:54] And so because of that we lie to the salespeople. We we perceptually take their attention off of what they think is happening and force them to at least consider over the next several days to try to see if things are actually different or not. And all of a sudden they start to see things differently again. You see how that works?
[32:14] So I'm not a fan of encouraging you to just have to lie to people, you know, or to or to have to do these kinds of things. But regardless, I mean, this is what makes a difference. Does that make sense to everybody, just as an example, I put that link in the chat, by the way, if you guys want to watch it later, you know, see see it in action.
[32:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "RECENCY BIAS" / "#5"
NÚMEROS: #5
VISUAL: Quinto ponto principal, conceito de viés de recência destacado, princípio psicológico fundamental
[32:34] But the uh yeah, the point I'm trying to make, and this is very important, okay, is look, salespeople, they operate off recency bias, okay? And recency bias is simple to understand. It's nothing crazy, okay? It's not some super advanced psychology.
[32:50] It's just what is most recently happening to them or what they think is most recently happening to them is what they will assume they're going to continue experiencing. Okay? And we got to we got to break that sometimes.
[33:04] So, as I mentioned in my case with that with that direct example I just provided to you, you got to tell them some shit changed so they can see it different. This is that lesson that Tony Robbins talked about.
[33:15] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Tony Robbins Red vs Blue Exercise"
NÚMEROS: 10 segundos
VISUAL: Exemplo clássico de atenção seletiva, exercício de cores vermelho vs azul, demonstração de foco direcionado
[33:17] If you guys have ever seen that video where Tony says, "Hey, look around your room real quick and and just in 10 seconds, okay? Tell me everything you see that's red." Okay? And then he gives the person 10 seconds and they're looking around the room and they're trying to see everything that's the color red.
[33:34] And then he asks them afterwards, he says, "Okay, your 10 seconds is up. You ready? Okay. tell me everything you saw that was blue. And then the person's like, "Oh, I didn't really see anything blue. You know, I saw a bunch of red stuff." And he's like, "Yeah, exactly. Because you were looking for red, you saw red."
[33:52] So that's all this is in in application of salespeople. Okay. They see and experience what they want or view as recently happening to them. And then they project that they project that out and assume it's going to keep happening.
[34:07] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Catch It Early"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando ponto de intervenção precoce vs intervenção tardia, conceito de prevenção vs correção
[34:09] And that's why we got to catch it. So to be clear, assume that keeps uh it's going to keep happening. Okay. So to be clear, like when a salesperson's in a slump, what do you think they're going to they're going to assume? Well, they assume that shit's going to keep happening the way that it's been happening. That's how they operate. So, we've got to we got to break that recency bias.
[34:29] So, when I when I give you the lesson of company narrative, okay, and I give you the example of what I just talked about Brandon Carter sales team in that video, which once again was completely made up and fabricated, but made all the difference to the sales team's performance, okay?
[34:46] And it turned everything around. Well, it's the it's the same kind of logic when it comes to recency bias. It's like we have to really monitor. Okay, this is this when in itself is is uh very important to monitor.
[35:00] If we if we undermine this and we aren't looking for it constantly, it can really get away from you and then all of a sudden your entire sales team's just in this fucked up place mentally, you know, and it takes a lot more effort and work to rebuild them back up rather than just maintain them in a great momentum and positive mental state where they think everything's going good. Okay?
[35:22] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Performance Dip Management"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Dois gráficos comparativos - intervenção precoce (pequeno dip) vs intervenção tardia (grande dip), custo de recuperação
[35:24] Okay, so you got to find those moments where you start to see, okay, like small dips, you catch it right here and you fucking swing it back around again. Okay, whenever you start to see small dips, you have to catch it. Okay?
[35:37] Because to be clear once again, if you don't I catching it down here, it takes an exceptional amount more work and you technically still have to go through more dip time frames versus, you know, and you're technically still not at the same point as you would have been if you just would have caught it right here instead.
[35:55] This is what's very important to understand once again for why we have to monitor it. If we fail to do so, then the dip can really fuck up the revenue of the organization.
[36:05] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Revenue Impact Example"
NÚMEROS: $600,000/mês para $200,000/mês
VISUAL: Queda dramática de receita representada, impacto financeiro de narrativa negativa não controlada
[36:07] I'm talking like I've seen organizations that go from like, you know, $600,000 a month to like $200,000 a month. And it's because within that month there was this cancerous perspective that spread throughout the sales organization. Okay? Can't allow that kind of stuff to happen.
[36:25] If you have sales management and they by chance are like watching this type of video or you're going to hand down these types of lessons to them, you have to understand how to train them on these things as well.
[36:36] Because if they're your foot soldiers that are out there monitoring these kinds of things and and they're trying to spot it while it's happening or during it like before it happens ideally or gets too out of control, they need to understand like they can't just be buddy buddy and one of the sales people, you know, like they have to operate the thing from above.
[36:55] If they're in the trenches and they're going through the same stuff, then you can't teach these kinds of lessons to your sales manager and you have to you have to assume this position yourself. Okay. Uh very important distinction.
[37:07] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "POSITIVE RECENCY BIAS = SCALE AGGRESSIVELY"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Conceito de momentum positivo, estratégia de scaling durante períodos quentes, aproveitamento de outbreak
[37:09] To be clear, playing into recency bias is also very important. Okay. This can be a very positive thing as well. So recency bias can positively benefit you in a lot of ways. Uh but the main one being when there's positive momentum you want to aggressively play into the positive momentum can positively impact as well. So when things are hot scale aggressively that's very important to understand scale aggressively.
[37:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Growth Pattern: Stagnation vs Outbreak"
NÚMEROS: "Várias centenas de milhares" em meses de outbreak
VISUAL: Gráfico mostrando padrão de crescimento - períodos de estagnação intercalados com outbreaks, oportunidades de scaling
[37:37] Okay this makes a big difference to the amount of revenue that you can bring in. It looks like this in terms of a graph, okay? You'll see periods of time that are like relatively stagnant and then all of a sudden you see like a small uptick.
[37:50] It's kind of like what we talked about last week where you can start to identify that there's an outbreak occurring. Okay? And when and if you recognize that, you immediately play into it. Okay? Like start spending more money. Load up the salespeople's calendars.
[38:06] Uh have them like record training for the organization during times where you are in periods like this. Okay. Uh understand that typically salespeople can be what gets you an additional several hundred,000 in a month or you know an extra tens of thousands of dollars in a month based on how well you play into their outbreak and their positive momentum periods of time.
[38:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Ideal Growth Pattern"
NÚMEROS: Várias centenas de milhares em revenue adicional
VISUAL: Gráfico ideal - linha ascendente consistente com outbreaks capitalizados, vs padrão errático com oportunidades perdidas
[38:30] Organizations will typically look like this where there's like stagnancy and then outbreaks and then stagnancy and then outbreaks then stagnancy and outbreaks. When they do this successfully in these periods of time, they are tacking on several hundred,000 in revenue versus during these periods of time, you know, these are like the normal months where there's not exceptional growth, but you know, the chart's still going up and to the right.
[38:54] You know, this is ideally what it looks like versus unfortunately what most businesses will experience. Like they'll get some average months, they'll have like a they'll have like a slight downturn unfortunately, then they'll have like an outbreak month and then like some average months and then like some slight down months, you know, and like unfortunately it just it just looks like shit through time.
[39:15] But thankfully, you know, their graph generally still trends up and to the right, you know, when you look at the average of the whole thing. But I mean there are some emotional periods where like you lose a fucking ton of money, you made a fucking ton of money, you know, and this this is just not an ideal way to do it.
[39:31] I much more prefer uh you know this type of system here where once again you are just successfully playing into the outbreaks that the business has. And you do that once again by mastering salespeople's recency bias because like I said when things are going well, what does that mean for a salesperson? Okay. When things are going well, that means things are going to keep going well. Critical to understand. Okay. Critical to understand, critical to master.

[40:28] Great question. So, first of all, minimum amount of video pitches that get sent per day. Might not sound like a ton, but for us, we get a really high view rate, a really high response rate as a result of this. We like to send at least 20 a day.
[40:44] Um, to be clear, that's the main stat that makes all the difference. We understand that our view rate stats and that our value driven follow-up stats also are very important. Our quantity of calls that we generate and our closing rates are very important.
[40:58] But once again, the main stat for us that I talked about here that makes all the difference is just quantity of video pitches that go out. Um, from there we track the watch rate, the response rate, the quantity of people that we successfully turned into a call. also like the the watch to call rate.
[41:17] From there, we track things like our show rate and our overall length of time that it takes uh to make that sale occur. Another important statistic that we track and quantify is just quantity of value driven follow-ups that go out.
[41:31] Um that also makes a substantial difference as we see a majority of our revenue come from valuedriven follow-ups. But it obviously all starts with we don't have people to follow up with. We're not sending out video pitches in the first place. Does that make sense?
[41:45] Yes, that makes sense. Thank you.

[41:54] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "RECAP - Core Principles"
NÚMEROS: 5 pontos principais
VISUAL: Resumo visual dos conceitos principais, estrutura de revisão organizada
[41:56] Okay. Cool. So, to be clear, just to recap so far, number one thing, once again, that makes the biggest difference to a sales team is the level of accountability that they have consistently through time. It should not go down. Okay? It should be consistent. Okay?
[42:14] Doesn't have to go up through time, but it but you need to once again you need to be self-aware of the fact that when you first start with somebody versus months later, you're typically dropping the level of accountability rather than maintaining it for what it was. Okay?
[42:29] It's harder to come back from a low level accountability and increase it through time than it is to just maintain it when it's high. Structure got to have proper sales management. I can't stress that enough. Okay? You don't got a sales manager in the team. You are the sales manager. Okay? assume the role.
[42:47] Daily sales training um to be clear doesn't always have to be completely new stuff. Doesn't have to be completely different styles. It is just encouraged essentially to repeat the core lessons because for some reason, no matter how many times we teach them, sometimes weeks later, it's not habitualized yet and it gets dropped out of their behavior.
[43:07] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Habituation Rate"
NÚMEROS: 5-10% implementam após primeira exposição
VISUAL: Estatística de baixa taxa de habitualização, necessidade de repetição, conceito de reforço constante
[43:09] So look at it like the quantity of people within your team that are going to successfully hear something once and then habitualize it is excessively low. I'm not going to make up a percentage, but I would I would truly imagine it's below like 5% maybe 10% of people that are going to hear something and just habitualize it right away.
[43:28] So, we have to repeat the same core lessons again and again and again. But don't do it every other day, you know, do it every few days or every few weeks. Just repeat the same things.
[43:38] Morning meetings, uh, end of day meetings, just kind of start the day properly framed or recap things at the end of the day. Those are all encouraged. Um, to be clear, this is not micromanagement. Once again, this is proper structure is how you have to look at it.
[43:54] If you think any of this is micromanagement, you probably have a low level of accountability within your organization by your own preference. Therefore, you have a fucked up structure in your organization as a result of that. That is losing you money in comparison to making you more money.
[44:10] That's why you're likely at where you're at. And that's why the big dogs out there that are sweeping you right now in revenue are making what they're making and you're not making that amount of money.
[44:21] Role playing can be huge because once again, if you have more than one salesperson and they're sharing with each other what's working, okay? And they're experiencing what the other person's experiencing. And once again, they get an opportunity to overcome that. That's like practice before a game.
[44:37] That's what all fucking professional sports players and sports teams do in history. They fucking practice before they go on the field. That's all the structure really is here, okay? It's just a structure for practice and a structure for the games. And once again, ideally increasing the frame that they have before they go through their day.
[44:55] Number three, once again, ideally you assume control slashresponsibility over their personal lives because their personal lives can impact their professional lives. You don't need to tell them what to do personally, but you need to monitor like, you know, obviously it's not good to do drugs like that's going to affect their professional performance.
[45:14] It's not good to go out there and consume a fuckload of alcohol or any alcohol for that matter, you know, that can affect their professional lives. It's not good to come into work hung over. You know, it's not good to go through a breakup and then expect to have peak performance, you know, on the following days.
[45:30] It's not good to eat excessive amounts of junk food or junk food in general. You know, how many of you guys have gone out to lunch in the middle of a workday, eaten some kind of food that gives you brain fog, you know, and it just fucked up the rest of your day.
[45:44] It's like that's an example of that. We want to ideally influence, you know, how our salespeople are operating in their personal lives to influence their professional lives and make the company more money ultimately resulting in making them more money too.
[45:58] By the way, to be clear, KPIs, there's typically a main stat that has to be tracked that makes all the difference for the rest of the stats. You track everything anyway. And ideally, you want to see when a salesperson's potentially going through an experience like this where they have a low amount of revenue coming in. Okay?
[46:17] When a salesperson has a low amount of revenue coming in or a low stat, uh you need to step in and you need to ask the person like, "Look, what's missing? What am I not doing that I need to do that's going to make all the difference for you here and make you more money?"
[46:32] You know, what do I need to train you on? And if they say, "No, I'm good." Well, great. I expect these stats to turn around then. You know, I don't expect them to stay the way they are. Two weeks of bad stats in a row, we got problems. Okay.
[46:46] Sales people need positive narratives. Once again, if a sales team is constantly saying to themselves like, "Oh, lead quality is bad. Fuck such and such is happening." You know, like people are not financially qualified. Get fucked with those narratives, okay?
[47:01] Take their feedback to be fair because sometimes it's accurate. I'm not saying there's no instance where a salesperson is just, you know, lying completely and saying like, "Oh yeah, everything's bad and it's actually good."
[47:13] There are instances, once again, where things are bad and they're bad and you need to take that feedback and work with it in some instances. Take it seriously. But once again, when there's contradicting data, like I talked to you about in the Brandon Carter example, where his entire application data analytic set showed us that it's the opposite of what the sales people were saying, we're going to take an opportunity to reframe the sales people by telling them it's a fuckload of changes and stuff so we can undo that narrative. Okay?
[47:44] And to be clear, if the sales people are going through a reality of a negative narrative and it is true, then obviously as marketers or as the company, you need to work to fix that narrative and then do the same thing. But, you know, obviously it'd be true in that example. You made some substantial changes. You did some things different.
[48:01] Uh, once again, you turned everything around. Just try to help them reframe their view or at least open the idea to them that there's a potential change that occurred. Okay, this is a critical part of the sales process.
[48:14] Um, once again, sales people operate off recency bias. So to be clear, they see they experience what they want and typically once again whatever they're recently experiencing is what they're projecting out as as this is going to be a continuation. This is what they're going to continue experiencing.
[48:31] Uh when you're able to catch these things prior to the to drop off happening, okay? We want to avoid the drop offs like this. Okay? We don't want that to occur. Ideally, once again, when we see a drop off start to occur, we catch it.
[48:45] We adjust what they are recently experiencing so they can project out a more positive and beneficial experience to the organization overall rather than just saying, "Oh yeah, this is just going to keep fucking happening and and things just perpetually get worse and worse and worse and you don't catch it as the business owner or the sales manager." Okay.
[49:05] Um to be clear, recency bias also has very positive impacts on the business when you play into it successfully. If if you don't want to experience something like this where you get a fuckload of ups and downs, but you're still generally trending up and to the right, then to be clear, what you have to do once again is you have to be able to identify when recency bias is positively impacting the organization.
[49:28] These things pop up in very simple ways. They're just people saying things in your sales team like, "Dude, lead quality is fucking awesome right now." You know, you see the amount of revenue the sales team's bringing in is great. All the feedback is very positive. Okay?
[49:43] And they're projecting out that that's going to keep happening. And ideally during those times you fucking floor it, okay? You fill up their calendars to the brim. You know, you push that organization to the limits and you try to make sure that you know you expand during that period of time and then stabilize it after. Okay?
[50:01] It's not going to maintain itself forever. But if you really press those outbreaks, you make a whole lot more money than you do if you just kind of laxidasically do the same stuff while recency bias is positively playing into what you're doing.
[50:15] So to be clear, this specific set of lessons, I mean, I could go fucking on and on and on about like littleer things, but these these all have major impacts and make a substantial difference to bringing sales people out of slumps, keeping them into a great frame and great state.
[50:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "SALESPEOPLE = THE DIFFERENCE"
NÚMEROS: Não especificado
VISUAL: Ênfase máxima na importância do departamento de vendas, posicionamento como crítico para organização
[50:34] And there's one last thing I just want to comment on before I close this out, okay? Because this this is also very important is look salespeople are technically in my opinion believe it or not as as a marketer saying this I know it's kind of wild but regardless it's true salespeople are the difference. Okay these people within this team cannot be disregarded.
[50:56] They cannot be like dismissed. Okay. You can't undermine the importance of these individuals. You you have to admit they are the gateway to the revenue coming into the organization. Like when you have sales people that are taking all the calls and bringing in the money like it is a critical department within the organization.
[51:16] It is it is in my opinion the most important department within the organization outside of marketing and leadership within the team. Okay. It is a a department that I feel like doesn't have the level of importance in most businesses labeled onto it as it should.
[51:33] So therefore, it doesn't get the level of attention and resources and time that it should. Okay? A lot of people just think like I can hire salespeople and just, you know, that if I try to find good ones like they'll be good. That's just not true.
[51:47] You know, you can't just hire sales people and just be hands off with them. that just not it just doesn't work. It has to be a highly focused on department, okay? Heavily focused on department that you put a lot of time, effort, and attention into to see a lot better results as time continues to pass.
[52:06] It's critical to do this. It's critical to think this way. And once again, it's critical to to not bloat the salespeople's egos in doing this specific step. But once again, it it should not be a department that's just dismissed. It should not be like their feedback is just dismissed.
[52:23] And once again, it should not be like all this stuff is just like something you're going to consider to do. I mean, dude, this stuff is critical to do. This stuff is like what's necessary to do to make a fuckload of money with your sales teams, okay?
[52:37] And uh to be clear, you know, I think they're the most like these people should be making the most money out of anybody else in the organization. Okay? They should be the they should be the most rewarded. They should be the most paid.
[52:50] Um to be clear, once again, they should like everything needs to be considered when you're talking about like incentive structures, you know, the culture of the sales team. like I just can't stress enough how important they are to an organization and how handinhand they are with you know marketing being successful or anything else in the business being successful.
[53:10] So anyway, that's uh that's about what I've got for us there on on everything uh when it comes to sales people. Does anybody have any questions or any comments or anything that you guys want to add to this? Any kind of additional perspective that you guys have or any kind of points you think I missed out on?
[53:27] Anybody. Cool. All right. Adrian said, "Solid training." I appreciate that coming from a coming from a sales manager. Thank you.
[53:37] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Q3 Mastermind Announcement"
NÚMEROS: Julho 20-21, 3 palestrantes confirmados, 30-35 pessoas esperadas
VISUAL: Anúncio de evento, lista de palestrantes, estrutura de mastermind
[53:39] All right. Cool. Well, if you guys don't got anything, then I guess that's it. Uh, we got our mastermind coming up soon. I will announce the speakers officially, but let me give you three ones that we've already confirmed that are coming. And I think we've got a nice stacked roster.
[53:54] Once again, Q3 masterminds July 20th and 21st. First of all, we've got Gelani. Uh, most of you guys, I don't think you know Galani. I worked with Galani back at Dan Lock's office. He was one of those guys that as as AI started surging in popularity, just dropped everything else he was doing and, you know, dedicated his entire professional career to AI.
[54:17] Every piece of content that Gelani produces is produced by artificial intelligence with some amazing tools. Um, out of everybody that I've seen talk about this and replicate themselves digitally to making content and having AI also do the editing for them, his shit is the most advanced, surprisingly out of everything else I've seen.
[54:38] So, I asked Galani, I said, "Can you get in front of us as a group and can you teach us how to essentially digitally replicate ourselves so we can all make content with AI, too?" He agreed to it.
[54:50] Um to be clear, I think that's going to be one of the best talks that we've had in a while that's very relevant to, you know, once again that essentially just using everything that's available to us currently in terms of tech. And uh I you'll be quite impressed when you when you see what he's got going on as well. And you'll you'll see the perspective that I have on that.

[55:42] Um to be clear organically is how he generates almost all of that revenue so I'm excited to bring him in front of the group and have him teach some of those lessons he's really big on Tik Tok and he he gets a lot of uh revenue from Instagram as well and he does it through a live webinar.
[55:58] So, I think that we're going to have a lot of value come from Mason.

NÚMEROS: Não especificado (Forbes mencionado)
VISUAL: Terceiro palestrante, especialização em press e mídia, foco em se tornar contribuidor
[56:04] Um, in addition to that, I got uh Alex Quinn, longtime inner circle student, specifically focused on press. Um, I have a really cool talk that I'm going to have him ideally do for us, which is he's a contributor on platforms like, you know, all all the big press platforms like Forbes is an example as one of the common places I see him get articles published all the time, but it's because he's a contributor on these platforms.
[56:30] So, he's going to teach us how to become contributors. So, we all essentially have our own press channels where we can write our own articles and shit publish them on places like Forbes and all the other popular press channels and it's a lot easier than what most people think.
[56:45] It's obvious, it's also very cost- effective in comparison to have to having to pay all the press guys. Um, to be clear, in addition to that, he has his own media outlet. He's going to talk to us a little bit about that and getting one of those started.
[56:59] And and lastly, we're actually gonna write an article in real time at the mastermind during his talk and we're going to submit it to get published on different channels.
[57:08] So to be clear, that's the alternative rather than having to be a contributor in most instances when you get press written about you. It's just something was written and then submitted onto different news wires or PR pre P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P P PR wires platforms where journalists can pick them up and include them in their stories or write entire stories about it.
[57:30] So, in real time at the mastermind, we're going to write an article and then we're going to publish that onto a platform where it could potentially get picked up. So, I think it's I think it's a pretty well-rounded uh very like tactical type mastermind.
[57:44] And usually the Q3 masterminds run a little lighter in terms of attendance. So, to be clear, if that's the case, since I never have you guys RSVP, we'll have two options, right? just a normal mastermind structure, which we've done, and then if it's a little lighter, like it was last year.

[58:26] So, I'm super excited for it. Hope you guys are, too. I'm excited to see everybody who comes out and per usual, if you got any questions on anything that we talked about today or in relation to anything else, just uh reach out, shoot me a DM. Happy to help.
[58:41] All right, cool. Talk soon, guys. Hope you enjoyed the training.
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
25 vendedores na organização Grant Cardone
6 vídeos de treinamento diários obrigatórios
20-30 minutos de treinamento matinal
30% aumento em show rate com selfie videos
30 minutos de role play diário
2-4 pessoas por pod de role play
1-2 horas de preparação matinal estruturada
$151,250 em payouts de trading (desde 15 de maio)
20 vídeo pitches por dia (KPI mínimo da agência)
Queda de $600K/mês para $200K/mês (exemplo de impacto negativo)
5-10% taxa de habitualização após primeira exposição
Várias centenas de milhares em revenue adicional durante outbreaks
Q3 Mastermind: Julho 20-21
30-35 pessoas esperadas no mastermind
Cardinal Mason: $600-800K/mês em receita orgânica
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
Accountability constante - não diminuir com o tempo
Estrutura diária obrigatória para vendedores
Treinamento de vendas diário - repetir lições comprovadas
Morning meetings - KPIs, wins, atualizações
Role play sessions - 30 minutos após meeting
Tracking de KPIs visual em parede
Sistema de intervenção precoce - catch dips cedo
Propaganda positiva - customer wins, sales wins, cultura
Controle sobre vida pessoal - macros, relacionamentos, saúde
Reframing de narrativa - video fabricado para quebrar negatividade
Scale agressivo durante momentum positivo
Selfie videos para aumentar show rate
Value-driven follow-ups como fonte principal de receita
Exercício Tony Robbins (vermelho vs azul) para demonstrar atenção seletiva
ESTRUTURA:
5 Pilares Principais:
ACCOUNTABILITY
STRUCTURE (daily training, meetings, role play)
ASSUME CONTROL OVER PERSONAL LIVES
POSITIVE NARRATIVE
RECENCY BIAS MANAGEMENT
Daily Structure (Grant Cardone):
Cardone University training (6 videos)
Morning meeting (company-wide)
Role play sessions (pods 2-4 pessoas)
Day framed to win (1-2 horas preparação)
KPI Tracking System:
Main stat identification
Weekly graphs impressos na parede
Intervenção em trending down
Reconhecimento público para trending up
Narrative Management:
Monitor constantemente
Catch negativity early
Reframe perception quando necessário
Amplify positive momentum
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Exemplo de impacto negativo: $600K → $200K/mês
Outbreaks podem adicionar várias centenas de milhares/mês
Cardinal Mason: $600-800K/mês orgânico
Jeremy trading: $151,250 em payouts (desde 15/5)
Custo de recuperação muito maior que prevenção
EXEMPLOS PRÁTICOS:
Brandon Carter: Tracking de macros, intervenção em breakup de vendedor
Grant Cardone: 25 vendedores, estrutura militar, KPIs na parede
Elon Musk: Intensamente envolvido em todos os níveis
Video fabricado: 0 mudanças reais, 100% mudança de percepção
Leo (sales guy): Trading matinal impacta performance do dia
PALESTRANTES Q3 MASTERMIND:
Gelani - Replicação digital com AI, conteúdo gerado por AI
Cardinal Mason - $600-800K/mês orgânico, TikTok/Instagram, live webinars
Alex Quinn - Press/Forbes contributor, criar próprio media outlet, escrever artigo ao vivo
DURAÇÃO: 58:41 FORMATO: Group coaching call / Training webinar com apresentação de slides e exemplos práticos
